draft.introduction: Chapter 15 of Machiavelli's The Prince (1513) marks the decisive break with classical and Christian political philosophy. Machiavelli declares that he will deal with politics as it actually is, not as it ought to be.

translation.section: Chapter 15: Concerning Things for Which Men Are Praised or Blamed
translation.text: It remains now to see what ought to be the rules of conduct for a prince toward subjects and friends. And as I know that many have written on this point, I expect I shall be considered presumptuous in mentioning it again, especially as in discussing it I shall depart from the methods of other people. But, it being my intention to write a thing which shall be useful to him who apprehends it, it appears to me more appropriate to follow up the real truth of the matter than the imagination of it. For many have imagined republics and principalities which have never been seen or known to exist in reality; for how one lives is so far distant from how one ought to live, that he who neglects what is done for what ought to be done, sooner effects his ruin than his preservation.

translator_notes.note: The phrase "follow up the real truth of the matter than the imagination of it" (la verita effettuale della cosa) is the founding statement of modern political realism. By rejecting imaginary republics—a pointed reference to Plato and Augustine—Machiavelli divorces political science from moral philosophy, creating the modern understanding of politics as an autonomous domain governed by its own logic of power.
